core_concept: Medieval Europe: Feudalism, the Church, and the Crusades
facts: Feudalism was a social hierarchy where lords granted land to vassals in exchange for military service, while serfs worked the land and were bound to it.
The Catholic Church was the most powerful institution in medieval Europe, owning about one-third of all land and influencing kings and peasants alike.
The Crusades were a series of religious wars launched by European Christians between 1095 and 1291 to reclaim the Holy Land from Muslim control.
The Black Death, caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, killed roughly one-third of Europe's population between 1347 and 1353, not by sword or famine but by plague.
After the Black Death, the shortage of laborers gave surviving serfs more bargaining power, which weakened the feudal system by allowing them to demand wages or move to towns.
A vassal was a noble who swore loyalty to a lord and received a fief, but unlike a serf, a vassal was free and could hold multiple fiefs from different lords.
misconceptions: Students often think the Black Death killed people instantly or only in cities, but it spread through fleas on rats and devastated rural villages as well.
Some students believe the Crusades were a single, short war, when in fact they were a series of eight major campaigns spanning nearly 200 years.
Students may think serfs were slaves who could be bought and sold, but serfs were legally bound to the land, not owned as property, and had some rights under manorial custom.
